topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The music swelled, a vibrant wave of joy washing over the ballroom. I clutched my lukewarm champagne flute, the crystal slick against my palm, and watched the happy couple dance across the floor. Then, a familiar silhouette caught my eye near the buffet table. It was Sarah. My Sarah. Her smile, bright and effortless, seemed to mock the knot tightening in my chest. Every muscle in my jaw clenched. I forced a polite, distant smile, my eyes flicking away before they could linger. When she turned, our gazes met for a fraction of a second—a collision of shared history and present awkwardness. A sudden, sharp awareness of the distance between us made my throat feel tight. I quickly turned towards a group of acquaintances, needing the solid anchor of friendly chatter to keep my composure.

[story 2]

The air in the reception hall was thick with perfume and celebration. I was trying to appear engaged in a conversation with Mark, nodding along to anecdotes about the bride’s family, but my attention kept snagging on a figure across the room. It was Alex. Seeing them, standing near the entrance, sent a cold tremor down my spine. My hands started to sweat, and I found myself gripping my glass so tightly I worried the condensation would crack the glass. When Alex looked my way, there was a flicker—a recognition that instantly flooded me with a profound sense of wrongness. I felt a desperate need to vanish, to melt into the wallpaper. I mumbled an apology to Mark, a clumsy excuse, and practically bolted toward the periphery of the room, the noise of the party suddenly sounding abrasive and overwhelming.

[story 3]

I’d spent the last hour meticulously rehearsing innocuous small talk, a flimsy shield against the unexpected. The sheer volume of the celebration was almost deafening, but all I could hear was the frantic drumming in my own ears. Then, there she was. Standing by the punch bowl, laughing with a group of old college friends, was Chloe. The warmth that usually accompanied seeing her evaporated, replaced by a sudden, heavy stillness in my limbs. I felt a distinct urge to retreat, to somehow erase the last few minutes from memory. When our eyes locked, I couldn't meet them. I focused intensely on a passing waiter, my breath catching in a shallow hitch. I managed a weak nod in her direction, a gesture so minimal it was almost invisible, before turning abruptly and finding a quiet corner near the balcony doors, needing space to simply breathe.
